guest_role: Partner at Lightspeed Venture Partners (former Head of Growth/Product at Slack)
assertiveness_why: Harry asks thoughtful, prepared multi-part questions and shares his own experiences/opinions as color, but he never contests or pressure-tests Mercy's claims.
question_style: Mostly open, curiosity-driven questions with frequent personal asides and callbacks to prior conversations with mutual contacts (Nicole, Jeremy); he shares his own views (e.g. on Twitter authenticity, async work) but rarely challenges her directly and does not interrupt.
tone: Very matey and informal, laced with self-deprecation ('this is so unfair of me', 'apologies for being broad') and genuine fandom ('I'd heard so many great things about this guest'); energy is warm and conversational, deviating fully from the prepared outline.
depth: Goes reasonably technical on SaaS/collaboration-tool metrics (NPS, DAU/MAU ratios, net revenue retention thresholds) and venture process mechanics (deal docs, Slack channels, post-mortems), tracking specific named tools like Roam Research and Superhuman.
harry_quotes: What are the certain challenges for you in making the switch?
Do you take notes on Evernote? Is it roam research?
Bluntly, would you advise founders to get good at Twitter?
learnings: VCs should treat their own judgment and network as the 'product' they iteratively improve, mirroring a startup's build-ship-adapt loop.
Losing deals to slower response time is one of the most common and avoidable failure modes for investors.
Net revenue retention above ~140% and DAU/MAU above 60% are strong signals of durable collaboration-software businesses.
Collaboration startups often fail by selling to managers (who hold budget) rather than the individual-contributor end users who actually adopt the tool.
Being active and authentic on Twitter functions as a real distribution advantage for cult-following consumer/collaboration products.
notable: Harry openly admits the conversation abandoned the prepared question schedule entirely and issues an on-air correction to a stat misstated in the intro.
